<p02
<p>Loads a content file containing animation keyframe data and resolves into animation data which plays in reverse, i.e. backwards.</p><p>Works the same as AnimFile except the direction of the animation data.</p><p>When specifying a sub frame range, the frame range is pulled from the animation file before reversal.</p><p>For a typical walk style animation with extracted motion, likely an AnimAlign to first frame of the original forward animation will be required.</p>

Additional Anim Files
Additional Materials
Additional animation files which will be merged into the loaded animation.  Animation channels will be overwritten by later files.  The last source file to define an animation channel (bone/morph) wins.  Earlier occurrences of that animation channel in subsequent animation files will overwritten.  The resulting animation frame count will be the maximum of all specified animation files.  Animation files which are shorter will have additional copies of their final frames added to the end for padding. 
Additional bones to pull SDF geometry from (will not articulate!)
Additional influence for the attachment.

Applies a bone scale set to the animation over the specified frame range specified by the 2D animation curve.<br>The Input Range is the X Axis range (horizontal) and corresponds to the frame range.<br>The Output Range is the Y Axis range (vertical) and is the scale set weight value over the frame range.<br>Any frames which fall outside of the frame range are left alone.<br><center><br><img src='game:tools/images/modeldoc_editor/docs_anim_markup_curve.png'/><br></center>AnimForceBoneScaleSet nodes are processed in the order they appear under the AnimFile node and will overwrite previously written data if the frame ranges overlap.<b>NOTE</b>: Although the Input/Frame Range are floats, integers should be used. Curve data is not evaluated at run time. The curve is sampled once per frame of the animation and the values are linearly interpolated between frames.<br>
Applies an animation markup over a specified frame range with a value controlled by an editable 2D animation curve.<br>The Input Range is the X Axis range (horizontal) and corresponds to the frame range.<br>The Output Range is the Y Axis range (vertical) and is the value over the frame range.<br>Any frames which fall outside of the frame range are left alone.<br><center>\n<table>\n<tbody>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Ctrl + Mouse Move</td>\n    <td>Snap Frames</td>\n  </tr>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Alt + Mouse Move</td>\n    <td>Snap Values</td>\n  </tr>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Wheel</td>\n    <td>Zoom</td>\n  </tr>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Ctrl + Wheel</td>\n    <td>Zoom X</td>\n  </tr>\n</tbody>\n</table>\n</center><br><center><br><img src='game:tools/images/modeldoc_editor/docs_anim_markup_curve.png'/><br></center><b>NOTE</b>: Although the Input/Frame Range are floats, integers should be used. Curve data is not evaluated at run time. The curve is sampled once per frame of the animation and the values are linearly interpolated between frames.<br>

Applies to any attributes
Applies to array parents or non-array items (just not array members)
Applies to morph animation only.<br><br>This is the per component precision threshold to try and attain when optimizing the bit rate. If the error is below the precision threshold, we will remove bits until we reach it without exceeding it. If the error is above the precision threshold, we will add more bits until we lower it underneath. 
Applies to skeletal animation only.<br><br>See :: https://github.com/nfrechette/acl/blob/develop/docs/compressing_a_raw_clip.md<br><br>The last important setting to choose is the error_threshold. This is used in conjunction with the error metric and the virtual vertex distance from the skeleton in order to guarantee that a certain quality is maintained. A default value of 0.01cm (or ~0.004 inches in game units) is safe to use and it most likely should never be changed unless the units you are using differ. If you do run into issues where compression artifacts are visible, in all likelihood the virtual vertex distance used on the problematic bones is not conservative enough.
Applies to skeletal animation only.<br><br>Specify how aggressively to attempt to reduce the memory footprint. Higher levels will try more permutations and bit rates. The higher the level, the slower the compression but the smaller the memory footprint. <br><b>NOTE</b>: Currently lowest, low and medium are all equivalent.
Applies to skeletal animation only.<br><br>https://github.com/nfrechette/acl/blob/develop/docs/creating_a_skeleton.md<br><br>The virtual vertex distance is used by the error metric function. It will measure the error of a vertex at that particular distance from the bone in object space. This distance should be large enough to contain the vast majority of the visible vertices skinned on that particular bone. The algorithm ensures that all vertices contained up to this distance will have an error lower than the supplied error threshold in the CompressionSettings. It is generally sufficient for this value to be approximate and it is often safe to use the same value for every bone in humanoid characters. A value of 3cm (or ~1 inch in game units) is good enough for cinematographic quality for most characters. While visible vertices around the torso will often be further away than this, finger tips and facial bones will be closer than that. Because the compressed track data is stored in relative space of the parent bone, any error will accumulate down the hierarchy. This means that in order to keep the leaf bones within that accuracy threshold, all the parent bones in that chain will require even higher accuracy.

Legacy Compatibility :: Ignores Pose Parameter and always blends with fixed_blend_val.\nSpecific to how the class CMDL : public IModelRenderHelper sets up pose parameters.\nInstead of 0, CMDL uses 0.5 as the default and also provides to API access to set the blend.\nThis results in all 1D/2D Blends rendered with CMDL using a fixed blend weight of 0.5.\nThe particle system uses CMDL to render models.\n

Loads an external mesh file and transfers its morph data (blend shapes) onto the parent mesh using a wrap deformer. The MeshOpStack vertex selection controls which vertices are affected when transferring morph deltas.
Local Angles
Local Attachment Offset
Local Drag
Local Force
Local Gravity CP
Local Hierarchy controls the space in which sub-frame interpolation occurs. Normally each bone will use parent space (local space) for sub-frame interpolation between two keyframes. Local Hierarchy allows the interpolation to occur in the space of a different bone or model space (world space). The space of a different bone is useful for switching the hand which is holding an objects, for example. Specifying no bone as the New Parent Bone will result in the interpolation occuring in model space (world space). This is useful if the bone becomes detached from the model, e.g. dropping a weapon. Local Hierarchy only affects how sub-frame interpolation between two key frames occurs. Local Hierarchy does not encode the bone differently at compile time.

On Interval
On True
On models with an animgraph, setting this to true sets the Activity Name to ACT_IDLE, false will clear the activity.  The intended usage is on models intended to also be used without an animgraph, i.e. as map entities like prop_dynamic with use animgraph disabled in Hammer.  NOTE: If Activity Name is anything other than empty or ACT_IDLE, this field is not writable.

OverrideVectorStream
Overrides the bone scale of the specified bone over the specified frame range specified by the 2D animation curve.<br>The Frame is the X Axis (horizontal).<br>The Scale is Y Axis (vertical).<br>Any frames which fall outside of the frame range are left alone.<br>\n<table>\n<tbody>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Ctrl + Mouse Move</td>\n    <td>Snap Frames</td>\n  </tr>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Alt + Mouse Move</td>\n    <td>Snap Values</td>\n  </tr>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Wheel</td>\n    <td>Zoom</td>\n  </tr>\n  <tr>\n    <td>Ctrl + Wheel</td>\n    <td>Zoom X</td>\n  </tr>\n</tbody>\n</table>\n</center><br><center><br><img src='game:tools/images/modeldoc_editor/docs_anim_markup_curve.png'/><br></center>Force Bone Scale nodes are processed in the order they appear under the AnimFile node and will overwrite previously written data if the frame ranges overlap.<b>NOTE</b>: Although the Input/Frame Range are floats, integers should be used. Curve data is not evaluated at run time. The curve is sampled once per frame of the animation and the values are linearly interpolated between frames.<br>
Overrides the target VSNAP resource (defaults to "particles/models/%model_name%/%vsnap_node_name%.vsnap"

Specials
Specific Physics Body to Replace
Specified
Specified class '%s' is a base class, @exclude cannot be used for base classes
Specifies a NmSkeleton that the model can be targetted by.
Specifies a material replacement to apply to all following models. Mapping goes from the material specified by the model( including material group selection ) to the replacement material. Previous material overrides are not considered when determining the base material.
Specifies that the specified motion should be removed from the specified bones.  If no bones are specified, motion is discarded from all root bones, i.e. bones without a parent.  Motion is discarded for the specified channels by copying the first frame over all frames of the specified motion.
Specifies the AnimGraph2 that the model will use by default.
Specifies the animation's inherent blend mode to worldspace, mutally exclusive with delta.<p>If <b>TRUE</b> the animation will <b>WORLDSPACE</b> blend itself onto the current animation stack.<p>If <b>FALSE</b> the animation will <b>BLEND</b> itself onto the current animation stack.<p><span style="color:#ffff00"><b>NOTE</b></span>: Animgraph animations should normally leave this at the default value of <b>FALSE</b>. The blend mode is set on the Animgraph node explicitly.
Specifies the animation's inherent blend mode.<p>If <b>TRUE</b> the animation will <b>ADD</b> itself onto the current animation stack.<p>If <b>FALSE</b> the animation will <b>BLEND</b> itself onto the current animation stack.<p><span style="color:#ffff00"><b>NOTE</b></span>: Animgraph animations should normally leave this at the default value of <b>FALSE</b>. The blend mode is set on the Animgraph node explicitly.
Specifies the materials that the model will render with by default. (Allows overriding the materials inferred from the source FBX/DMX file.)
Specifies the maximum number of blend weights per vertex on this mesh
Specifies the name of the attribute in the material whose value is to be set. This is the name which appears in the 'Attributes Used' section when editing an expression in the material editor.
Specify a tangent-space normal map (source texture) that will contribute to the object-space output.
Specify an ambient occlusion map (source texture) to multiply over the generated cavity map.
Specify that root motion should be extracted from this animation, if no root bone is specified, the first bone without a parent is used
Specify that the animation markup child nodes for this animation should be processed in the order they appear.
Specify the framerate at which to play back the animation.  It is filled in from the data in the animation file so normally it doesn't need to be modified.
Specify the initial frame range:
Specify the local hierarchy frame range:
Specify the motion type.  Choices are as follows: <br><b>Single</b>: Extract motion from the first frame of the animation.<br><b>Uniform</b>: Extract motion from every frame in the animation.<br><b>Specified</b>: Specify the frame ranges with which to extract motion.<br>
Specify the type of culling of the skeleton for animated models.\t\t\t<br><b>NOTE</b>: Models without animations are always Aggressive.<br>\n\t\t\t<table>\t\t\t<tr>\t\t\t<th align="left"><b>Aggressive</b></th>\t\t\t<th align="left">Removes any bones which are not skinned or referenced</th>\t\t\t</tr>\t\t\t<tr>\t\t\t<th align="left"><b>Leaf Only</b></th>\t\t\t<th align="left">Removes any bones which are not skinned or referenced that have no children</th>\t\t\t</tr>\t\t\t<tr>\t\t\t<th align="left"><b>None</b></th>\t\t\t<th align="left">Doesn't remove any bones</th>\t\t\t</tr>\t\t\t</table>
Specify whether the animation loops.
Specify whether the animation should be hidden from the compiled view outliner on the right and pulldowns.
Specify which specific shapes belong to this detail layer(s)<br><br><em>(NOTE: All other shapes will <strong>NOT</strong> belong to these detail layers unless explicitly included)</em>

The <b>Parent</b> animation constraint is a little different in that it expects the bone to be constrained along with any offset to be specified directly on the node itself rather than via an output child node.  Also to note is that the rotation offset on the node is specified as an Euler rotation in degrees in XYZ order rather than the QAngle everywhere else.  If the <b>Constrained Bone</b> field is empty and there is an output child node, the first output node will be used.  If the <b>Constrained Bone</b> field is not empty, all output child nodes will be ignored.

\n\t(\t@\t&\t>\t&\tK\t8\t 
\n\t2\tM\t/\t>\t(\t
\n\t\tCREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS Thumbnails (\n\t\t\tAssetPath TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n\t\t\tFingerprint INTEGER,\n\t\t\tPNGData BLOB\n\t\t);\n\t
\n\t\tDELETE FROM Thumbnails WHERE AssetPath IN ( SELECT AssetPath FROM AssetsToDelete );\n\t\tDROP TABLE AssetsToDelete;\n\t\tCOMMIT;\n\t
\n\t\tDELETE FROM Thumbnails WHERE AssetPath IN (\n\t\t\tSELECT T.AssetPath\n\t\t\tFROM Thumbnails T\n\t\t\tLEFT JOIN ExistingAssets EA\n\t\t\t\tON T.AssetPath = EA.AssetPath\n\t\t\tWHERE EA.AssetPath IS NULL\n\t\t\t\tOR T.Fingerprint != EA.Fingerprint\n\t\t);\n\t\tDROP TABLE ExistingAssets;\n\t\tCOMMIT;\n\t
\n\t\tINSERT INTO Thumbnails ( AssetPath, Fingerprint, PNGData )\n\t\tVALUES ( ?, ?, ? )\n\t\tON CONFLICT( AssetPath ) DO UPDATE SET\n\t\t\tFingerprint = excluded.Fingerprint,\n\t\t\tPNGData = excluded.PNGData;\n\t
\n\t\tPRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;\n\t
